name: Courgette
notes: Productive medium-early variety with dark green striped fruits, excellent flavour and firm flesh. Bushy, compact habit. Can be harvested as baby courgettes, traditional size, or left to grow into marrows. Watch for slugs when young. Hungry feeders — add plenty of organic matter and water/feed regularly. Frost sensitive.

name: Lettuce
notes: Attractive cos-type lettuce with savoyed purple-brown and green leaves. Larger than its name suggests. Slow to bolt and stands well in warm conditions outdoors. Harvest whole or use as cut-and-come-again. Possible synonym of Red Leprechaun / Red Cimarron — may date to the 18th century. Space 30cm apart each way.

name: Tomato
notes: Early maturing determinate bush tomato. Slightly pinkish fruits with a sweet/acid balance and fruity flavour. Tumbling habit — excellent for hanging baskets, pots and small spaces. Can be grown under cover or outdoors. Does not require side-shooting. Sow in seed tray with light covering of compost in warm conditions, early March. Allow 30cm between plants.

name: French Bean
notes: Dwarf French bean producing an abundance of stringless pods on compact plants around 50cm tall. No need for supporting structures, though bamboo or hazel supports are helpful when heavily laden. Keep picking regularly to promote more flowers and beans. Sow in succession from late spring to late summer for a longer harvest. Rich soil and warmth preferred.

name: Kale
notes: Russian-type kale with flat, frilly leaves in blue-green with strong purple ribbing. Grows a couple of feet high and wide. Very hardy through winter; becomes large and bushy in spring before flowering. Dual crop possible — harvest young shoots like baby purple sprouting broccoli before flowers form. Leaves are sweet and nutty. Originally from Garden Organic's Heritage Seed Library. Space 75cm apart.

name: Tomato
notes: Indeterminate cherry tomato. Needs support canes or string. Pinch out side shoots for best yield.

name: Courgette
notes: Prolific cropper. Harvest fruits when small for best flavour. Watch for powdery mildew in late summer.

name: Broad Bean
notes: Autumn-sown variety for overwintering. Very hardy. Watch for blackfly in spring — pinch out shoot tips.

name: Sweet Pea
notes: Highly fragrant heritage variety. Deadhead regularly to prolong flowering. Sow in autumn or late winter.

name: Chilli
notes: Medium heat, excellent for pickling. Needs a long warm season — start early indoors. Can overwinter indoors.

name: Basil
notes: Classic pesto basil. Pinch out flower heads to keep leaves coming. Protect from cold and draughts.

name: Sunflower
notes: Tall statement plant, great for birds. Direct sow after last frost. Stake in exposed positions.

name: Lettuce
notes: Compact cos-type lettuce. Sow successionally every 3 weeks for a continuous supply. Bolt-resistant.

name: Sweet Pepper
notes: German heirloom 'Leibesapfel' variety well-suited to Welsh climate. Produces squat, ribbed fruits with thick, juicy, sweet and mild flesh. Early-maturing — ripens even in dull, wet years. Continues producing into late autumn. Grow in greenhouse or containers. Feed weekly with comfrey or seaweed feed.

name: Chilli Pepper
notes: Heatless Capsicum baccatum chilli that bears prolifically in the Welsh climate. Produces numerous small, bright red ripple-textured fruits. Eat like sweet peppers. Sow seeds 0.5–1 cm deep, keep above 21°C. Transplant when 10–13 cm tall, 45 cm apart.

name: Lettuce
notes: Dark red loose-leaf/butterhead type with large heads of broad, frizzy-edged leaves. Crisp sweet inner hearts with more savoury outer leaves. Slow to bolt. Harvest as baby leaves cut-and-come-again or allow to mature into full heads. Sow March–July; harvest May–October.

name: Poppy
notes: Stunning Papaver somniferum of unknown origins. Reaches approximately 5 feet in height. Self-seeds annually. Sow into a pre-watered bed in autumn as poppies require a cold period before germination. Keep moist until germination. Remove seed heads before ripening to prevent self-seeding.

name: Carrot
notes: Sweet, crispy carrots with no discernible core — bred in America (1983) to contain three times the beta-carotene of standard carrots. Excellent raw or cooked. Direct sow outdoors April–July in fine, stone-free, well-drained soil. Thin to ~8 cm. Watch for carrot root fly.

name: Tomato
notes: Delicious indeterminate cherry tomato in deep red and black hues with a sweet, rich flavour. Sow indoors mid-February to end of March at 18–22°C. Requires tall supports; pinch out side shoots regularly. Best grown under cover but can crop outdoors.

name: Scabious
notes: Dark, velvet-like pincushion flower heads on long stems — perfect for cut flowers and bouquets. Blooms from late September until first frost. Sow in modules from March, pot on as needed. Tolerates light frosts. Does not like being pot-bound; plant out as soon as it outgrows its pot.

name: Winter Squash
notes: Small Hubbard-type winter squash producing 2–3 blue/grey teardrop-shaped fruits per plant. Dense, bright orange flesh with a distinctive chestnut taste. Excellent storage. Sow in modules mid-April to early June at 18–21°C. Plant out at 3 true leaves after hardening off. Full sun required for ripening. Cure 1–2 weeks before storing at 10–15°C.

name: Winter Squash
notes: Popular and very reliable traditional winter squash. Old variety that stores well and produces several large fruits on vigorous vines. Sweet, nutty, bright orange flesh that roasts well. Start in heated propagator at ~23°C. Give lots of light once emerged. Space 90 cm apart with generous organic matter.

name: Pea
notes: Delicious soup pea originating from the Clydach Valley in Wales. Vigorous plants reaching ~6 feet tall with smooth yellowy-green seeds. Multi-purpose: eat fresh, as mangetout, or dry for soup — makes the best mushy peas. Direct sow or start in modules, sow March–May. Harvest August–September.

name: Beetroot
notes: Well-known for reliable, consistent performance and resistance to bolting. Deep red, ring-free flesh with sweet and earthy flavour. Excellent for roasting, pickling or raw use. Sow in trays/modules March–June or direct from April at 2 cm depth. Space 10 cm apart in rows 30 cm apart. Ready approximately 6 weeks after sowing.

name: Climbing French Bean
notes: Beautiful climbing bean with a slight red stripe and great flavour. Originating from Rouen, France (1950s). Vigorous ex-commercial variety with delicate white flowers. Versatile for curries, stews, salads; surplus can be canned or frozen. Sow April–June; harvest July–September. Direct sow 2 seeds per cane, 30 cm spacing, or start in modules.

name: Borage
notes: Sometimes called Starflower. Bright blue star-shaped flowers attract bees and are edible with a cucumber-like taste. Reaches ~80 cm. Self-seeds readily. Does not like transplanting — sow outdoors in well-drained bed in mid-spring. Sow 5 cm apart, thin to at least 30 cm.

name: Tomato
notes: Gorgeous indeterminate tomato — tastes like a tomato should, delicately sweet with a slight smoky hint. Elongated, grape-shaped fruits (~100 g) displaying black, green, purple, orange and red. Holds well on the vine. Bred by Brad Gates of Wild Boar Farms, California. Sow indoors mid-February to March at 18–22°C. Pinch side shoots; requires strong supports.

name: Runner Bean
notes: Beautiful black-seeded runner bean brought back from near extinction. Pods up to 20 cm long — a very fine culinary bean. Sow indoors in modules mid-spring; transplant after frost, spacing 25–30 cm apart. Requires support frames with canes or string. Red flowers. Botanical name: Phaseolus coccineus.

name: Tomato
notes: Large, deeply ribbed bright red tomatoes with an interesting tangy flavour when cooked with spices. Irregular, slightly flattened and curvy shape. Originally sourced from a market in Yangon by Adam Alexander (The Seed Detective). Best grown as cordon under cover. Sow mid-February to March at 18–22°C; germination within 14 days.

name: Winter Squash
notes: Productive early butternut variety suited to smaller spaces — spreads only ~4 feet wide. Yields ~4–5 fruits per plant at ~2 lb each. Bright orange flesh with sweet, nutty taste. Early-maturing moschata type better suited to Wales's shorter growing season. Sow modules mid-April to early June at 18–21°C. Full sun for ripening; cure 1–2 weeks, store at 10–15°C.

name: Winter Squash
notes: Trailing delicata-type winter squash with extremely thick flesh, small seed cavity and rich, sweet, almost date-like flavour. Peach/tan with dark green stripes; highly variable size (small loaf to ~3 lb). Open Source Seed Initiative pledged variety, bred by Carol Deppe. Matures in 90 days. Sow mid-April to early June at 18–21°C. Harvest before frost; store at 10–15°C.

name: Runner Bean
notes: Welsh heirloom runner bean with red flowers and beautiful long, slender pods. Seeds are pink and black when mature. Seed has been grown in Wales since 1961 — a stonemason's family variety. Vigorous and resilient in challenging Welsh summers. Sow April to early May in modules 5 cm deep; harden off before planting out. Requires strong support.
colour: pink and black seeds, red flowers

name: Carrot
notes: Short, stumpy French heirloom carrots from the 19th century. Work well in containers and poor soils. Sweet and crisp with traditional, 'carroty' flavour. Direct sow April–July in situ in fine, stone-free soil. Shallow drills ~13 mm; thin to ~8 cm. Can overwinter in ground or store in sandy soil.

name: Radish
notes: Beautiful globe-shaped, bright red radish with crisp, fresh and mild mustard flavour. Ready in 4–6 weeks from sowing. Sow directly in rows 15 cm apart, February–August. Thin to 2 cm apart. Sow every few weeks for continuous supply. Great dipped in butter and salt or lightly pickled.

name: Chinese Violet Cress
notes: Also called February Orchid (Orychophragmus violaceus). Vibrant green leaves with intensely violet flowers that last for months. Harvest leaves, flowers and stems. Plants grow bushy to ~60 cm. Prefers full sun; will not tolerate shade. Autumn sowings flower from late February. Sow thinly in modules or direct after frost; transplant when large enough.

name: Coriander
notes: Aromatic culinary herb prized for fragrant foliage in curries, salsas and soups. Can also be grown for seed as a curry spice. Sow directly outdoors from May in 1 cm deep drills with 30 cm row spacing, or start in modules/pots from March. Sow September for a winter supply under cover.

name: Cosmos
notes: Easy-to-grow annual daisy-like flower in shades of pink, purple and white. Tall foliage with prolific flowers — great cut flower. Sow indoors February–May at 16°C; transplant at 50 cm spacings after last frost. Later sowings can be direct sown into warm soil. Deadheading encourages longer blooming.

name: Sweet Pepper
notes: Classic red pepper with thick sweet flesh producing good numbers of blocky fruits ~10 cm wide. Sweet, crunchy and juicy — versatile for salads, BBQ, curries, pasta and stir-fries. Long growing season; start in propagator at 25–30°C late January to March. Plant in larger pots or greenhouse beds by May. Feed weekly.

name: Cucumber
notes: Hardy round outdoor cucumber with delicious crisp, crunchy texture. Rambling growth habit — suitable for ground or low supports. Sow late April to early May under protection at 20°C+; transplant to fertile soil after last frost. Space ~80 cm apart. Harvest regularly.

name: Luffa
notes: Vigorous climbing gourd (Luffa aegyptiaca) producing natural sponges. Harvest young and green for eating, or leave to mature and dry to reveal a natural scrubbing sponge — fully compostable. Start indoors at 20–25°C; transplant to greenhouse or warm sheltered spot after frost. Requires sturdy support.

name: Tomato
notes: Early/mid-season red-yellow striped tomato with thin skin and excellent flavour. Produces generous trusses of mid-sized round fruit. Bred by Dr. Lewis Darby in the 1960s. Thin skins mean no peeling needed for canning. Indeterminate vine — requires strong supports and side-shooting. Best grown under cover. Sow mid-February to March at 18–22°C.

name: Chilli Pepper
notes: Classic kitchen chilli with good heat and great flavour. Striking red peppers up to 15 cm long — harvest green or red. Plants reach ~60 cm, excellent yields. Early ripening makes it reliable in short Welsh summers. Sow late January to March in propagator at 25–30°C. Plant in larger pots or greenhouse by May. Feed weekly.

name: Climbing French Bean
notes: Welsh adaptation of the Italian borlotti bean. Well suited to cool, wet Welsh climate. Tall and bushy with lime green leaves, lilac flowers, dark purple-splashed pods. Dried beans have velvety smooth texture — excellent in soups, casseroles and chillies. Heritage Seed Library variety, originally gifted by a Cardiff resident. Start April in modules; plant 15–20 cm apart after frost.

name: Tomato
notes: Brand new blight-resistant tomato variety bred by Simon Crawford specifically for outdoor cultivation — first released 2025 through The Gaia Foundation's Seed Sovereignty Programme. Semi-determinate, open-pollinated. Good blight resistance for outdoor growing even in challenging climates. Sow March–April indoors; transplant May–June in sunny spot spaced 60 cm apart. Requires support.

name: Tomato
notes: Tall vine tomato producing loads of beautifully sweet, zingy cherry-sized fruits. Originally bred in Pembrokeshire by Tony Haigh at Brithdir Mawr Community. Indeterminate — requires strong supports and regular side-shooting. Sow mid-February to March at 18–22°C. Plant out end of April. Feed and water regularly.

name: Dwarf French Bean
notes: Rare drying variety bred in the 1970s by Professor Colin Leakey. Produces white beans with wonderful creamy texture — perfect size for baked beans; historically rated by both Heinz and Raymond Blanc. Compact plants ~25 cm tall. Sow modules undercover late April; transplant 20–25 cm apart when frost danger passes. Harvest August/September.

name: Pea
notes: Late maincrop heritage pea bred and introduced in 1895. Hardy and reliable with signs of real drought resistance and no signs of mildew. High yields of plump, sweet peas. Plants 120–150 cm tall produce elongated pods of 8–11 peas. Eat fresh, freeze or dry. Sow March–June, 3 cm deep, 3–4 cm apart. Requires sturdy supports.

name: Tomato
notes: Vigorous vine tomato with exceptionally long double trusses of larger-than-average cherry tomatoes. Welsh heritage variety — grown in Cathays, Cardiff for over 40 years and reportedly grown at Highgrove. Sourced by Adam Alexander (The Seed Detective). Best under cover but can be grown outdoors. Sow mid-February to March at 18–22°C; germination ~14 days. Requires tall supports and side-shooting.

name: Cress
notes: Unusual broad-leaved heritage cress (Lapidium sativum) giving a peppery bite to salads. Can be used as a baby-leaf crop or left to form larger plants in cool spring/autumn. Dates back to the early 19th century; proven well-suited to Wales's cool, wet climate. Sow sparingly in shallow drills in early spring or autumn. Thin to 15 cm. Bolts quickly in summer.

name: Winter Squash
notes: Delicious nutty-flavoured squash with orange flesh in manageable 1–2 kg fruits. Highly productive; stores well into February. Versatile for roasting, risottos, nut roasts, curries and soup. Sow in modules mid-April to early June at 18–21°C. Full sun for ripening. Harvest before first frost; cure 1–2 weeks; store at 10–15°C.

name: Pea
notes: Don't be fooled by the name — Harold Idle is one of the hardest working peas in the garden. Tall, vigorous heritage climbing pea reaching ~2 m with white flowers. Each pod contains 9–10 sweet peas. Reliable for fresh consumption or freezing. Sow March–April in 5 cm modules or direct; transplant 7 cm apart. Requires sturdy wire mesh or tall pea sticks.

name: Tomato
notes: Cherry tomato producing very long trusses of exceptionally sweet and tangy fruit. Small, glossy, bright red fruits. Indeterminate vine — requires strong supports and regular side-shooting. Best grown under cover. Sow mid-February to end of March at 18–22°C; germination typically within 14 days. Plant out late April.

name: Parsley
notes: Vigorous flat-leaved parsley with large, dark green leaves and excellent flavour. Grows rapidly once established with high yields. Perfect for stews, soups, salads and sauces — also wonderful in chimichurri. Sow March–July; plant at 20 cm spacing. Harvest May–October.

name: Chilli Pepper
notes: Early maturing and very productive chilli. Mild to medium hot (2,500–5,000 SHU). Dark green, maturing to red fruits. Sow seeds 0.5–1 cm deep; keep above 21°C. Transplant to individual containers when seedlings have at least two pairs of leaves. Plant 45 cm apart indoors, root ball 1.5 cm below soil surface.

name: Pea
notes: Easy-to-grow sugarsnap pea with white flowers reaching 1.2 m. Plump, curved, juicy pods — sweet with a satisfying crunch, popular with children. Eat raw, steamed or stir-fried. Sow March–April under cover in root-trainers; plant out when seedlings reach 10 cm+. Space 7–10 cm between plants, 30–40 cm between rows. Provide supports.

name: Red Orache
notes: Vibrantly pink young tender leaves for salads; larger red leaves can be used like spinach. Grows tall when flowering — a captivating ornamental plant. Sow in April in trays or direct, 5 mm deep. Space ~10 cm for salad leaves; thin further for spinach-sized leaves. Allow to bolt and flower for ornamental effect.

name: Leek
notes: Modern, fast-growing leek variety. Can be harvested early or left to stand well over winter. Leaves are mid to dark green; shanks strong, dense and creamy white with mild flavour. Winner of Wales Seed Hub's leek trials. Sow indoors early spring or outdoors mid-to-late spring. Transplant when 20 cm tall and pencil-thick, spacing 20 cm apart in 15 cm deep holes.

name: Climbing French Bean
notes: Originally from the Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew. Beautiful purple-pink flowers complemented by purple-tinged leaves and stems, followed by flat purple pods with excellent flavour. Dried beans have a rich, nutty taste. Heritage Seed Library variety. Sow April–June; harvest July–September. Direct sow 2 seeds per cane, 30 cm apart, or start in modules.

name: Salad Onion
notes: Vigorous high-yielding Japanese bunching salad onion (Allium fistulosum) that grows well in the UK climate. Mild onion flavour; long white stalks and green foliage. Perennial — can be grown annually or divided to expand patch. Sow August–September for November–March harvests, or March–April for summer/autumn yields. Space 15 cm apart.

name: Purple Sprouting Broccoli
notes: Great hungry-gap crop. Late-maturing open-pollinated variety producing lots of delicious purple spears over a long period from March until May. Sow mid to late spring in modules; transplant 4–6 inches tall at 75 cm spacing. Prefers partial shade and well-drained soil. Protect from cabbage white butterfly. Feed with comfrey or nettle tea.

name: Climbing French Bean
notes: Introduced in 1885 by W. Atlee Burpee Co with a buttery taste. Earned its name because pods remain stringless for quite a while. Suitable for cooking whole or drying. Start in April in modules; transplant 15–20 cm apart after frost. Requires support structures; wind anti-clockwise.

name: Chilli Pepper
notes: High-yielding Capsicum baccatum chilli with a citrusy flavour. Fruits mature to bright yellow, ~10 cm long. Fantastic for making harissa and fermented chilli sauce. 30,000–50,000 SHU. Sow 0.5–1 cm deep; keep above 21°C. Plant 45 cm apart indoors when 10–13 cm tall.

name: Pea
notes: Exceptionally good Welsh heritage variety. Vigorous plants ~1.5 m tall producing plentiful plump pods of unusually large, sweet and delicious peas. Seeds brought to Wales by a German prisoner of war during WWI who worked on the Llanover estate near Abergavenny. Sow March–April in 5 cm modules; transplant 7 cm apart. Requires strong supports.

name: Tomato
notes: Incredible beefsteak tomato with very few seeds — pure flesh to eat. Heritage variety originally from Serbia via Lulu. Indeterminate vine. Sow indoors mid-February to end of March at 18–22°C; germination within ~14 days. Plant out late April with organic matter; strong supports required. Pinch out side shoots.

name: Climbing French Bean
notes: Reliable and versatile climbing French bean grown and saved by allotment holder Syd Melbourne for over 30 years before donating to Dyfi Valley Seed Savers in 2010. Originally from Sussex, adapted in Wales. Creamy white flowers; prolific crops of short, tender green beans. Mature seeds work dried for soups and casseroles. Sow April–June; harvest July–September. 2 seeds per cane, 30 cm apart.

name: Climbing French Bean
notes: Welsh-adapted borlotti climbing bean with 25+ years of climate selection by Maggie Carr. Sweet and tender when young; mature beans steam well; dried beans have nutty flavour for winter storage. Reaches 3 m — requires strong tall supports. Sow modules late April/mid-May; space 15–20 cm post-frost. Wind anti-clockwise.

name: Spinach
notes: Versatile, heavy-cropping spinach with deep green, fleshy leaves and superb texture. Excellent bolt resistance for extended harvesting. Sow March–September in trays/modules or direct at 2 cm depth. Space 15 cm apart in rows 30 cm apart. Ready ~6 weeks after sowing. Sow monthly for succession harvests.

name: Lettuce
notes: Fast-growing, flavourful and sweet Little Gem-type cos lettuce with crunchy, tightly closed hearts in neat bright green heads. Resistant to Downy Mildew, tipburn and bolt. Sow March–October; harvest May–December. Plant at 20 cm spacing. Direct sow or sow in modules.

name: Dwarf French Bean
notes: Modern early-cropping cultivar producing long, stringless beans of good flavour — begins cropping ~5 days earlier than comparable varieties. Beans held above plant for easy harvesting. Tolerant of Bean Mosaic Virus 1 and adverse weather. Bred by Kultursaat. Sow undercover late spring; direct sow 4 cm deep, 15 cm spacing, 40 cm between rows. Requires support when heavily laden.

name: Pea
notes: Tasty and productive round-seeded pea growing ~1.5 m tall — needs strong support. White flowers with green veining; sweet pods. Sow early March to mid-May, 2–3 cm deep. Can also be sown in early August as a catch crop. Space about 8 cm apart. Succession sowing recommended. Dig in organic matter before planting.

name: Pea
notes: Compact dwarf pea reaching ~50 cm — no support structures needed. Produces full pods of sweet peas. Direct sow ~2.5 cm deep, 4 cm between seeds, 45 cm between rows. October–November for early spring harvest; March–April for summer/autumn yield. Young shoots edible in salads.

name: Cucumber
notes: Reliable and tasty outdoor cucumbers suited to Welsh conditions. Small spikes brush off easily; thin soft skin — ideal fresh in salads. Harvest at ~8–10 cm for optimal sweetness and crunchiness. Keep harvesting and they keep producing. Sow late April/early May in greenhouse or windowsill; plant out when frost risk over and plants have 3–4 true leaves.

name: Nigella
notes: Love-in-a-Mist (Nigella damascena). Pale blue and white blooms with delicate misty foliage. Reliable, upright bushy annual to 50 cm — tolerates a range of conditions. Produces attractive seed pods. Great cut flower. Sow in modules indoors February–June with fine compost covering; or direct outdoors from April in rows 30 cm apart. Can also be sown in September to overwinter for early spring flowers.

name: Parsley
notes: Sturdy and reliable classic curly parsley with tightly ruffled leaves. RHS Award of Garden Merit winner. Compact and bushy — suppresses weeds and performs well in containers. Tolerates light frost. Milder flavour than flat-leaf parsley. Can take up to a month to germinate — patience required. Sow outdoors mid-spring or indoors earlier. Full sun or partial shade.

name: Pea
notes: Heritage pea with lovely bi-coloured pink and maroon blooms producing deep aubergine-purple pods. Dark pod colour aids harvest visibility. Young pods make crisp mangetout; mature pods reveal bright green, sugary peas inside. Vigorous tall climber. Sow early spring (March–April) in 5 cm modules; transplant 7 cm apart. Wrinkled seed type — needs warmer soil.

name: Pea
notes: Very large black-eyed bean-like pea for drying — similar to a Norwegian pea used by fishermen to make pea porridge. Plants reach ~2.1 m with attractive pink/purple flowers. Sow early spring in modules or direct. Provide sturdy supports, especially in windy sites. Harvest when pods are dry and papery.

name: Mustard
notes: Large-leaved Vietnamese mustard (Brassica juncea) — a wonderful addition to the early salad garden. Sweet with a delicate peppery kick. Flower buds can be eaten in salads before blooming. Sow spring (February–March) under cover; prick out and harden off before planting 10–15 cm apart. Sow outdoors midsummer to mid-September for later crops. Overwinters with protection.

name: Climbing French Bean
notes: Golden treat worth chasing up the canes! Sunshine-yellow pods that retain their colour when cooked. Clean, nutty and smooth flavour — string-free even at maturity. Highly productive. Mature pods yield small white beans for stews. Ornamental as well as productive. Sow indoors late spring or direct once frost has passed. Sturdy support required. Harvest pods young for tender eating or let some ripen for drying.

name: Winter Squash
notes: Heritage cultivar with fine golden flesh and sweet, nutty flavour — grows well in Wales. Average weight 3–4 kg, often up to 45 cm long. Originally grown by Cherokee Indians in the Appalachian Mountains; documented since the 1800s. Large trailing plant needing substantial space. Sow on edge in 8 cm pots during late April/May with heat. Space at least 1 m apart. Stores until Christmas when properly dried.

name: Calendula
notes: Large, double brilliant orange calendula flowers — ultimate 'plant it and forget it' hardy annual. Thrives in poor soils with exceptional drought tolerance. Attracts bees and hoverflies. Petals harvested for culinary garnish. Direct sow April–May where plants will flower, 1 cm deep, thin to 30 cm. Reaches maturity in 6–8 weeks. Partial shade acceptable. Autumn sowing possible for earlier flowering.

name: Dwarf French Bean
notes: Compact plant producing striking green pods splashed with purple. Harvest young for tender eating or leave to mature for drying — beautifully speckled beans perfect for soups or bean salads. Notably hardy and dries well even in wet Welsh weather. No staking needed. Sow after last frost in sunny spot with well-drained soil.

name: Climbing French Bean
notes: Stunning heritage climbing French bean — as beautiful as it is delicious. Striking bi-coloured seeds resembling a Yin Yang symbol. Young pods eaten fresh, but excels as a shelling or drying bean. Cooked seeds develop wonderfully soft, creamy texture akin to a floury potato or sweet chestnut — ideal for cassoulets and winter stews. Sow April–June; direct 2 seeds per cane, 30 cm apart, or modules for transplanting.

name: Tomato
notes: Sweet and delicious heritage cherry tomato containing high vitamin C levels. Named for its association with an amino acid believed to help relieve anxiety. Indeterminate vine producing long fruit trusses with slow ripening to extend harvest season. Sow mid-February to March at 18–22°C; germination ~14 days. Transplant late April; strong supports and side-shooting required.

name: Melon
notes: 17th century French charentais-type melon producing intensely fragrant, sweet, delicate orange flesh from compact vines. Early-maturing. Start mid-April to early May in heated propagator; transplant into greenhouse or polytunnel late May to early June. Space ~90 cm apart. Can sprawl or be vertically supported.

name: Mustard Greens
notes: Delicately frilly purple-leaved mustard with a spicy flavour. Cut young to add colour, form and flavour to salads; larger leaves excellent in a stir-fry. Sow August–October for autumn/winter harvest; February–April for spring/summer crop. Direct sow in rows 15 cm apart. Bolts in hot, dry conditions — keep well watered.

name: Tomato
notes: Mid-season indeterminate vine tomato. Dark aubergine on shoulders merging to bright green at the bottom, slowly turning pink when ripe. Heart-shaped salad-sized fruits with considerable variation. Delicate, wispy leaves with purple tinting. Deep maroon flesh delivers rich, umami flavour — excellent fresh or cooked. Sow mid-February to March at 18–22°C. Vigorous — exceeds 8 feet; strong supports and side-shooting essential.

name: Lettuce
notes: Beautifully attractive deep red oak leaf lettuce with a sweet flavour. Begins mottled green and red, deepening in full sun. Harvest as baby leaves, cut-and-come-again, or full-sized heads. Slow to bolt. Sow March–July; harvest May–October. Plant at 20 cm spacing.

name: Chilli Pepper
notes: Prolific Capsicum pubescens producing large, very hot bright red chillies — 4 cm box-shaped with distinctive black seeds. A different species to most UK-grown chillies, with hairy stems/leaves and blue-violet flowers. Originally from the Andes — thrives at higher altitudes and shows superior cold tolerance. Perennial — prune back and overwinter indoors. Space 50 cm apart.

name: Pea
notes: French heritage mangetout producing huge, tasty, crispy stringless pods — eat raw, steamed or stir-fried. Harvest when peas just start showing. Plants reach ~1.8 m with pink and magenta flowers. Sow directly April to mid-May (or early August as catch crop), 2–3 cm deep, 5–6 cm apart. Requires bird netting and sturdy support structures.

name: Tomato
notes: Bright red salad tomato with a pretty little pointed tip. Sweet and flavour-filled with firm flesh that bursts excitingly in the mouth. Plants produce abundantly with good fungal resistance. Indeterminate vine — tall supports and side-shooting required. Sow mid-February to end of March at 18–22°C; germination within 14 days. Plant out end of April.

name: Lettuce
notes: Reliable oak-leaved lettuce with dark red foliage. Grown from early spring to late summer for a succession of crisp and tasty leaves. Suitable for cut-and-come-again harvesting. Sow February–July in modules or direct; outdoor performance is best. Plant full-sized lettuces 30 cm apart each way. Water regularly to prevent bolting.

name: Snapdragon
notes: Easy-to-grow cottage garden Antirrhinum majus in mixed colours (white, light pink, hot pink, red). Tall stems — bold shapes and colours that look wonderful in a bouquet. Sow February–March in trays under cover with heat; prick out at 5 cm spacing. Plant out at 25 cm spacing when ~5 cm tall. Flowers June–September.

name: Sweet Pea
notes: Classic Spencer sweet pea with large, ruffled, exceptionally fragrant flowers. Vigorous annual climbers reaching ~1.8 m — ideal for covering fences or walls. Stunning palette from delicate creams and light pinks to rich mauves and deep purples. Flowers profusely from summer to early autumn. Sow autumn (3 seeds per 3" pot) or direct outdoors in spring drills. Pinch out growing points to encourage bushiness.

name: Runner Bean
notes: Prize-winning exhibition Welsh heirloom runner bean — king of the show bench throughout the 1970s–80s. Produces an abundant crop of deep green, slender pods over 40 cm long. Named after Brython Stenner from Cefn Cribwr, south Wales. Reselected from 'Enorma'. Start indoors late spring; plant out early summer when soil is warm, spacing 25–30 cm apart. Requires firm support. Pick before beans become too large.

name: Strawflower
notes: Striking Xerochrysum bracteatum in soft pinks and magenta. Excellent as fresh or dried flower — keeps its bright colours when dry and is remarkably long-lasting, often going into November. Plants reach ~75 cm. Sow March–May in trays under cover with gentle heat, lightly covering with compost. Prick out at 5 cm spacing. Plant out at 30 cm spacing after frost risk passes. Flowers July–October.
